speaker: interviewer
text: --1st, 1996. We left off on the last tape-- you mentioned that you were shipped back to the United States in January of '46. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, actually, December '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: December '40--

speaker: interviewee
text: December '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: '45. And your rank at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was second lieutenant.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was discharged in Dix. And we had a chance to enlist in the reserve. I, through interrogation and talking with German prisoners and other refugees, knew there will be another war with Russia. It turned out it'd be Korea instead. But I decided the next time I go into the-- to war, I'm not going towards the front line. I want to reserve a desk. So I stayed   in the reserve.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain in the reserves?

speaker: interviewee
text: For 20 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you discharged?

speaker: interviewee
text: I retired from the reserve in 19-- 60-- '73.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your rank at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: '71, sorry-- '71. Lieutenant colonel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what awards did you receive during the time you were in the service?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got the bronze star, as I mentioned, at the end of the war. And when I retired from the reserve, I was rewarded-- awarded the Army Commendation Medal. Of course, I have four-- five battle stars.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do after you came back to the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to my old job. And as soon as possible, I wanted to go to college. I used the record, which I fraudulently obtained from the Newark School of Engineering, to go-- get into Stevens Institute of Technology. And I stayed there for four years, became an engineer-- married in between.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your wife's name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hilde.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kaufman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married in January of '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were introduced by a mutual friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you first came to America, the very first time, were you able to talk about any of your experiences at all to other people about-- Jew and non-Jew-- about what had happened to you? Did you talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. For most people, either they heard so much about it-- they were sick of it; or they were not interested. And in between, there were-- there was nothing in between.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we have two children. They are--

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Thomas and Barbara. And they are married. And each one has two boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandchildren's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: From our daughter-- whose children-- the older is Leon and Alan. And our son's children are Richard and Matthew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughter-in-law's name is? Your daughter in-law's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wise.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barbara. Nope-- daughter-in-law, oh, sorry. Our daughter-in-law is Jane. Her-- her last name is Hagen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your son-in-law's name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wise-- Harold Wise.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you work as an engineer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the time I worked at Bell Laboratories, in Whippany. But I-- before I had a few other jobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you present-- what are you presently doing right now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. And-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: You are retired.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'm very successful at it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live for most of the years that you worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: For most of the years we lived in New Jersey and around New York-- the suburbs.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to Arizona?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 2 and 1/2 years ago when our son got a job here. And we decided-- I like warmer climate.

speaker: interviewer
text: What accomplishments are you most proud of?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't accomplish much, but actually, I am more proud of my military records than of my civilian record-- if that is an indication. Because in my civilian life, I was always handicapped. I had, number one, an accent. And as a professional, I was 10 years older than the average man of my qualification and experience. And that always held me back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were most proud of your military accomplishments.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, about my feeling about all of that. We hear a lot about the Holocaust and how bad it was, for it's the truth, which it definitely was. I certainly did not want-- do not-- do not want to distract from any of the sufferings-- which I realize is great-- but we were not the only one who suffered. Let me tell the story of our trustee from Dachau. His name was Elloris Stangle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you say that name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stangle-- S-T-A-N-G-L-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elloris-- Elloris. And he was a simple-- barge-- had on the Danube. And one day, he got drunk-- and in a bar, was very uncomplimentary towards Hitler. And he had a brother-in-law. His-- his-- his sister's husband was a ardent Nazi. So he wound up in Dachau. And that was four years before, and he was still there. There was a brief period where they discovered crab lice in our barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they discover?

speaker: interviewee
text: Crab lice-- scabies. And then we were insulated-- or isolated-- for two days, three days. And we got more personal. And I talked to him, and we complained bitterly about our fate, which it wasn't anything of pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, what are you talking about? You are here for six weeks, three months. I have been here for four weeks. From those who came six weeks ago, 1/2 of them are already released. I have not a chance of getting out of here alive, but I am sure my brother-in-law will see to it that he will never see me. So what-- so it was not only the Jews who suffered. And he told us a little bit about the history of Dachau. Dachau, as I mentioned before, was founded in 1933, right away. And in 1936, it was rebuilt for the primary purpose of taking care of the falloff from the Reichstag.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from 1936 till 1938, to the Kristallnacht, there were no Jews in Dachau. They were all political prisoners-- Germans-- fellow Germans. And so it wasn't only the Jews who suffered. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes, I know there was latent anti-- anti-Semitism in Germany. It was all over Europe. It's all over the world Yes, I know Hitler got into power with a majority of votes. But in order for Hitler to do what he wanted to do, a majority wasn't good enough. He had to get total support, total allegiance, and everybody had to march in the same step. And that is primarily what the concentration camps were for, and were used for that. We were just a-- a byproduct. And one of the biggest crimes in Germany was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This-- this crazy-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- disparaging-- it's a kind of-- it was a political crime punished-- punishable by a concentration camp, which was outside the regular jurisdiction of the German government. They had each a traditional system which would take care of all the criminals, whatever is there, and then, of course, illegal things. And here comes the concentration camps, where outside their jurisdiction they could do what they pleased. And if they didn't like what you said, you got there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, it's a big difference between the Jews and the others, where the Jews were released. I was released under the conditions that I leave Germany in six months. And when-- once I was out there, I could tell as much as I wanted about if they listened. So-- and the rest of the world, including the United States, they say, oh, it's only Jews-- who cares? And that was the impressions they could find.

speaker: interviewee
text: However, the Germans in the concentration camp, when any family member was put in the concentration camp, the family had to be super Nazis. They had to be very careful not to talk to anybody who might be politically not correct. Their children couldn't talk about their parents, and they could not be seen next to a Jewish kid in the school or anything. I think there was one fellow. I know there was one fellow in the-- in class. His father was sent to Dachau-- to concentration-- but he was away. He-- I don't know what was-- he was away. He was particularly careful to stay away from me. He wouldn't come to me in sympathy. And-- so-- all the-- and there were strict censorship of mail, of telephones, whatever it is. The Germans, if you had a family member in the concentration camp, they could not write abroad about that somebody was there. It was strictly forbidden. And if finally, the Nazis were ready to release him, they said, now, you will be released under one condition-- that you do not talk about it. As soon as you talk about it, you come right back here, and you won't get out. So it was completely hushed up that we are Germans. And as I said before, the impression created was the impression they wanted to create, and that was a screen with which they could prepare for the war without too much interference from the ally. You had a German Bund. You had, in the United States, you had similar organizations all over the world, who tacitly approved of what Germany was doing, who influenced-- influenced the government in the war. One of the greatest German supporters was Joseph P. Kennedy. As this is known. And he wasn't the only one. But Hitler wanted to create that impression that everything else within Germany is fine, and it's only the Jews, the vagrants, homosexuals-- there's others-- and to-- who keeps it-- to keeps the streets clean. Nobody really knew the-- it was-- the extent of the Jews in the concentration camp wasn't known. But less known, much less known, was the extent to really the way the Germans were treated there. I said, Dachau was for-- for two years without a single Jew-- just as an example. And when the war broke out, nobody cared anymore what went out, or that nothing went out anyway. Hitler had achieved what he wanted to achieve. And he used the Jews. He used homosexuals, the gypsies, whatever it is, as a cover. We were-- and as such, we were very-- he was very successful. And that shouldn't be overlook. Once Hitler came to-- into power, to keep such a tight lid on his own people, he needed lots of people. There was-- it wasn't just a few people on top who said, do that and that. There were hordes of henchmen who had to be there-- volunteers. And the biggest shame of the Germans is that Germany was able to have so many henchmen. And even nowadays, they don't talk about it. For everybody in Germany, whatever they thought, to some extent were-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --involved. I-- I can't think of the word. And so that's interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they don't-- nobody wants to talk about it. They couldn't talk about it before. They couldn't talk about it afterwards, because their neighbors also lived in-- lived-- they had to still live with neighbors. They didn't know who was who. So it became-- it was hushed up. And that is probably the last one-- is the basis on which some claim that the Holocaust never existed, because you hear the stories only from non-Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But that has to be considered, and it's very easy to say that the Germans who set out all, or not all, or whatever it is. The biggest crime, as I see, is what the Germans did with themself. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because it was-- not worse. We did suffer. I think it was-- for a German-- it was more insidious. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you understand what I'm trying to talk at?

speaker: interviewee
text: And actually, that is a message we should portray, I think. That's the way it really was inside Germany. It was-- it-- we were maybe-- the Jews-- were the last act in the big drama. But there were other acts just as bad. And just to see this-- and they may come again. That is the worst. You-- you can-- I see the first signs of recurrence with a combination of East Germany and West Germany. The East Germans will not rest till they are the top dogs. And we will have the same conditions, or the same field as we had before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So first, the Prussians became Germany in 1971 when they crowned the-- their king emperor of Germany. And that ended in Versailles, 50 years later. It's now 50 years since World War II. Something else may happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all I have to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Why do you feel-- or do you have any thoughts or speculations-- as to why you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Luck-- strictly luck. At that time, there was no extermination at the camp. The Holocaust-- it's the Final Solution. It's that-- that's the word I was looking for. The Final Solution was determined in Warnemunde, in 1942. Before that time, Hitler had hoped to get the Jews out of Germany, and to be able to confiscate his-- their property, and everything else, without a blood bath. For that was-- it didn't do him any good. And after the outbreak of war, Hitler even tried to barter the Jews with-- with a British-- with the ally. And it was Churchill who refused it-- to-- to take the refugees. For he was afraid there might be some spies in them. That's the excuse, I think-- and only after that, when the Final Solution in Warnemunde was from-- promulgated.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Do you have dreams at all about anything you experienced?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I have nightmares.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the-- about everything. I still have them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, can you describe one?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. See, I've had enough without describing [LAUGHS] them. And I'm afraid. Especially tonight-- I will have a few more. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any advice for your children, your grandchildren, for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It wouldn't do any good anyway. I-- there's nothing I can say. All I can say is watch out. And that's it. That's it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very much for the interview. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to untie me? Who is the picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a wedding picture of my parents, Isidor Selling and Ida, nee, Laub. It was taken 1911.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Anspach.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- it was part of the stuff we brought along.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my father, Isidor Selling, when he was in the army, during World War I. And did--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was taken in Anspach, or wherever. This is a picture of Hugo Laub. This is a brother of my mother, who was in World War I-- wounded twice. And when he finally-- his Germany didn't exist anymore, he committed suicide. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of our house in Lehrberg. That's where we were born and where we lived till we were pushed out of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That probably was taken shortly after World War I. This is a picture of the street where we lived. Our house is hidden. It was taken before 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lehrberg. This is a picture of my mother, and with my twin brother and me. It was taken shortly after our birth, in 1918.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say the names, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name was Leo Selling.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, Ida Laub.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where are you, on the left or on the right, as we're looking at this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm on the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken when we were about seven years-old, in Anspach, which is near Lehrberg. I'm the one on the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was on--

speaker: interviewee
text: My--

speaker: interviewer
text: --the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over my right, that's my twin brother, Leopold.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my passport with which I emigrated from Germany, in 1939. It was issued in Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is that letter in the upper left corner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of-- it's a J-- means I'm Jewish. And as you see, the name we were all forced to take-- the Jewish name-- is in the-- in my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name that-- that you were born--

speaker: interviewee
text: Isidor-- no, no, Israel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the data on my passport. The picture was taken shortly after I came out of Dachau. And you can see my hair is still cropped.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a immigration visa to England through the refugee camp, Richborough, in-- and a-- to-- the inscription says I'm only allowed to stay in the camp and cannot travel in England. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a shot of the refugee camp in-- which was taken in 1939, from a water tower nearby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in-- Richborough, England-- Kitchener camp. And it started out as a military camp in World War I, and it was-- and it was converted to a military camp in World War II.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my brother, Leopold Selling and me, before I went overseas from Newark, New Jersey, in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you in this picture-- on the left or right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm in uniform. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture of my two cousins, Katie Selling and Itzak Selling, whom I met up with while I was in the army. They came from Stutthof. They were in an extermination camp in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- that was in Fuerth, where they had a-- an apartment, where they could live for a while. This is the grave of my parents, Isidor Selling and Ida Selling, and the cemetery in Anspach. And that was a restored cemetery. The only thing surviving were granite stones, which could not be used for building material. Also, sandstones were destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken shortly thereafter, about 1950. This was taken in Fort Benjamin Harrison, in 1970, where I was executive officer during the summer training. At that time, my rank was lieutenant colonel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my daughter and her family. On the left is her youngest son, Alan, and her husband, Dr. Arnold Wise, then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Leon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon, he-- it was at the time, when he received-- was inducted into the honor-- National Honor Society. And on the right is our daughter, Barbara.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken last year, in 1995. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the family of our son, Thomas. On the left is our son, Thomas Selling. Next to him is his-- their eldest son-- the heck--

speaker: interviewer
text: Richard.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Richard, Martin.

speaker: interviewee
text: What? Richard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And next to him is our younger son, Matthew, and his wife, Jane Hagen.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're on.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife and helpmate, Hilde. We have been together for 48 happy years. But I'm not looking forward to another 48.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: 50 would be enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
